q: What is LADCI (Language Delay and ADHD/Cognitive Impairment with or without Cardiac Arrhythmia)?
a: A rare genetic syndrome causing language delay, ADHD or cognitive impairment, and sometimes heart arrhythmia
d: A common behavioral disorder caused by nutritional deficiency
A viral infection affecting brain development
A bacterial infection causing inflammation of the brain
A metabolic disorder affecting energy production in the brain
An allergic reaction causing neurological symptoms
A lifestyle condition related to screen time exposure
A hormonal disorder affecting childhood development
A purely psychiatric disorder with no genetic basis
e: LADCI is a rare genetic syndrome caused by mutations in a single gene. It is characterized by intellectual disability or language delay, ADHD-like symptoms, and in some cases cardiac arrhythmias. Understanding rare genetic syndromes helps clinicians identify and manage complex multi-system conditions.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna (genetic syndrome). Distractors improved.

q: What is PEBEL (Progressive Early-onset Encephalopathy with Brain Edema and/or Leukoencephalopathy)?
a: A rare progressive genetic brain disease causing early-onset encephalopathy with brain swelling and white matter damage
d: A bacterial meningitis causing acute brain inflammation
A viral encephalitis caused by herpes simplex virus
A metabolic disorder causing gradual liver and brain failure
An autoimmune encephalitis caused by antibody attack
A brain tumor causing progressive neurological decline
A nutritional deficiency causing white matter damage
A traumatic brain injury causing progressive degeneration
A cerebrovascular disease causing repeated small strokes
e: PEBEL is a rare, severe genetic encephalopathy characterized by progressive neurological deterioration beginning in infancy or early childhood. It involves brain edema (swelling) and/or leukoencephalopathy (white matter disease). It is caused by mutations affecting mitochondrial function or cellular maintenance.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna (genetic brain disease). Distractors improved.

a: A genetic form of male infertility caused by failure of sperm cell production
d: A hormonal disorder causing low testosterone without affecting sperm production
A structural blockage of the vas deferens preventing sperm release
A sexually transmitted infection damaging sperm quality
A nutritional deficiency reducing sperm motility
A varicocele causing elevated scrotal temperature
An autoimmune condition where antibodies attack sperm
An acquired condition caused by radiation or chemotherapy
A lifestyle-related condition caused by obesity or smoking
e: Spermatogenic failure (SPGF) syndromes are genetic disorders causing male infertility through failure to produce sperm (azoospermia) or producing insufficient or abnormal sperm. SPGF16 is caused by a specific gene mutation affecting the spermatogenesis process.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna (genetic infertility syndrome). Distractors improved.

q: What is MRT57 (Autosomal Recessive Mental Retardation 57)?
a: A rare autosomal recessive genetic syndrome causing intellectual disability
d: An X-linked genetic disorder causing intellectual disability in males only
An acquired cognitive deficit caused by traumatic brain injury
A form of intellectual disability caused by early childhood neglect
An autosomal dominant syndrome with variable expressivity
A trisomy syndrome like Down syndrome
An intellectual disability caused by intrauterine infection
An intellectual disability caused by premature birth
A metabolic disorder causing progressive intellectual decline in adults
e: Autosomal recessive intellectual disability (ARID/MRT) syndromes are caused by homozygous or compound heterozygous mutations in a single gene. MRT57 is caused by mutations in SETD5 or related genes. Unlike autosomal dominant forms, both parents carry one copy of the mutation without being affected.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna (genetic syndrome). Distractors improved.

a: A rare genetic syndrome characterized by intellectual disability, overgrowth, and distinctive facial features
d: A rare metabolic disorder causing progressive liver failure
A rare autoimmune disorder attacking the nervous system
A congenital viral infection causing developmental delay
A rare nutritional deficiency causing growth and learning problems
A chromosomal deletion syndrome with multiple organ defects
A hormonal disorder causing excessive growth and intellectual disability
A mitochondrial disease causing progressive muscle and brain dysfunction
A lysosomal storage disorder causing intellectual disability and organ enlargement
e: Shashi-Pena syndrome is caused by mutations in the ASXL2 gene. It is characterized by intellectual disability, overgrowth (macrocephaly, tall stature), and characteristic facial features. It belongs to a group of Mendelian disorders of the epigenetic machinery affecting chromatin regulation.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna (genetic syndrome). Replaced useless distractors with medically coherent syndrome alternatives.

q: What is PEBAT (Progressive Early-onset Encephalopathy with Brain Atrophy and Thin Corpus Callosum)?
a: A rare genetic brain disease causing progressive encephalopathy with brain atrophy and thinning of the corpus callosum
d: A non-genetic encephalopathy caused by chronic alcohol exposure
A viral encephalitis causing brain atrophy in adults
A vascular dementia causing progressive cortical thinning
An autoimmune encephalitis causing bilateral brain atrophy
A nutritional deficiency causing demyelination and brain atrophy
A traumatic brain injury causing corpus callosum damage
A bacterial meningitis causing long-term brain atrophy
A chromosomal trisomy causing corpus callosum hypoplasia
e: PEBAT is a rare autosomal recessive genetic encephalopathy. The thin corpus callosum (the main white matter tract connecting the two brain hemispheres) suggests a defect in axonal development or myelin formation. The condition typically presents in infancy with seizures and developmental regression.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 changed from immunity_disease to genetics_dna (genetic brain disease). Replaced distractors with medically coherent alternatives.

q: What is lethal congenital contracture syndrome 11?
a: A severe genetic syndrome causing fatal joint contractures present from birth
d: A non-lethal form of arthrogryposis causing joint stiffness from birth
A metabolic disorder causing muscle contractures in early childhood
A viral infection causing joint inflammation in newborns
A nutritional deficiency causing neonatal muscle weakness
An autoimmune disorder attacking fetal joint tissues
A complication of premature birth affecting joint mobility
An X-linked syndrome causing contractures only in males
A mitochondrial disease causing muscle stiffness and weakness
e: Lethal congenital contracture syndromes (LCCS) are a group of severe autosomal recessive disorders characterized by immobile fetal joints (contractures), leading to fetal akinesia (lack of movement). They are typically fatal before or shortly after birth. LCCS type 11 is caused by a mutation in a specific gene affecting neuromuscular development.

a: A genetic disorder causing abnormal left-right arrangement of the internal organs
d: A disorder causing symmetrical mirror-image organs (situs inversus totalis)
A congenital heart defect without organ position abnormalities
A disorder of organ rotation during fetal development without position change
A metabolic disorder causing multi-organ failure in infancy
An autoimmune disorder attacking multiple internal organs
A chromosomal disorder causing cardiac and splenic defects
A disorder caused by maternal diabetes affecting fetal heart development
A syndrome caused by ciliary dysfunction affecting respiratory mucus clearance
e: Heterotaxy (situs ambiguus) is a condition where the internal organs are arranged abnormally — neither in their normal left-right positions nor in a perfect mirror image. It is associated with complex congenital heart defects, asplenia or polysplenia, and biliary abnormalities. HTX8 has an autosomal inheritance pattern.

a: The progressive deterioration and death of nerve cells or their connections
d: The regeneration of nerve fibers after injury
The formation of new neural connections (neuroplasticity)
The myelination of nerve axons during development
The temporary suppression of nerve signaling by anesthetics
The normal pruning of unused synapses in childhood
The death of glial support cells without neuron loss
The repair of damaged myelin by oligodendrocytes
The growth of new neurons in adult neurogenesis
e: Nerve degeneration (neurodegeneration) involves the progressive loss of neuron structure and function. It underlies diseases like Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, ALS, and multiple sclerosis. Causes include genetic mutations, oxidative stress, protein misfolding, mitochondrial dysfunction, and inflammation.
i: MAJOR FIX: statement was hallucinated garbage ('nerve degeneration is a gene'). Completely rewritten with correct question, answer, and explanation. l2 changed from anatomy_organs to brain_neuro. Distractors fully replaced with neuroscience-appropriate alternatives.

a: A syndrome causing inflammation of the ileum, cecum, and ascending colon in neutropenic (immunocompromised) patients
d: An inflammatory bowel disease primarily affecting the colon in healthy adults
A bacterial food poisoning causing acute gastroenteritis
A parasitic infection causing chronic intestinal inflammation
A viral gastroenteritis causing diffuse intestinal inflammation
An autoimmune colitis caused by antibodies attacking the colon
A form of colitis caused by excess stomach acid
A hereditary inflammatory bowel disease with childhood onset
An intestinal obstruction caused by bowel adhesions
e: Neutropenic enterocolitis (also called typhlitis) typically occurs in severely neutropenic patients undergoing chemotherapy. The cecum and ascending colon become inflamed, and the condition can progress to necrosis, perforation, or sepsis. It is a medical emergency requiring broad-spectrum antibiotics and sometimes surgery.
i: Rewrote vague question/answer. l2 immunity_disease is appropriate (immunocompromised condition). Distractors replaced with GI-specific alternatives.
